clinicalStem: A 58-year-old male smoker with hypertension presents to the emergency department with a 90-minute history of crushing central chest pain radiating to his left arm, associated with diaphoresis and nausea. On examination: BP 140/90 mmHg, HR 95 bpm, RR 18/min, SpO2 96% on air. His ECG shows 3 mm ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF with reciprocal ST depression in leads I and aVL. Troponin I is pending. What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
explanation: ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF with reciprocal changes in I and aVL is the classic ECG pattern of inferior STEMI, caused by occlusion of the right coronary artery (or less commonly the left circumflex). The combination of crushing chest pain, radiation to the left arm, diaphoresis, and this ECG pattern confirms the diagnosis. This patient requires immediate reperfusion therapy (primary PCI within 90 minutes).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Anterior STEMI would show ST elevation in leads V1-V4, not in the inferior leads II, III, and aVF.
Inferior STEMI is correct — ST elevation in II, III, aVF with reciprocal depression in I and aVL is the hallmark pattern.
NSTEMI does not produce ST elevation on ECG; it presents with ST depression, T-wave changes, or a normal ECG with a positive troponin.
Unstable angina causes no permanent myocardial damage and does not produce ST elevation; ECG may show transient ST depression or T-wave changes.
Acute pericarditis causes diffuse (saddle-shaped) ST elevation across multiple territories without reciprocal changes, and is not territory-specific.
clinicalPearl: ST elevation in II, III, aVF with reciprocal depression in I and aVL = inferior STEMI; always check right-sided leads (V3R, V4R) to exclude right ventricular involvement.
keyClue: ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF with reciprocal ST depression in I and aVL.
commonExamTrap: Confusing the territory of ST changes — anterior STEMI affects V1-V4, not the inferior leads.
missedConcept: Territory-specific ST elevation patterns and their corresponding coronary artery distributions.
redFlagReason: STEMI is a time-critical emergency requiring immediate reperfusion (primary PCI within 90 minutes); delayed diagnosis risks extensive myocardial necrosis and death.

clinicalStem: A 65-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes and hyperlipidaemia presents with a 4-hour history of severe chest tightness at rest. She denies radiation to the arm or jaw. On examination she looks pale and clammy; BP 130/80 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, SpO2 98%. Her 12-lead ECG shows 2 mm ST depression and T-wave inversion in leads V4-V6, but no ST elevation. What single investigation is MOST CRITICAL at this stage to differentiate NSTEMI from unstable angina?
explanation: High-sensitivity troponin (hsTnI or hsTnT) measured at presentation and repeated at 1-3 hours is the key investigation to distinguish NSTEMI (positive troponin indicating myocardial necrosis) from unstable angina (negative troponin with no necrosis). Serial measurement is essential because a single early sample may be negative in the first few hours of myocardial injury. In NSTEMI the troponin rises and falls, whereas in unstable angina there is no troponin rise.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Echocardiography may show wall motion abnormalities in ACS but cannot biochemically distinguish NSTEMI from unstable angina and is not the immediate differentiating test.
Chest X-ray is useful to exclude pulmonary oedema or aortic dissection but does not differentiate NSTEMI from unstable angina.
Serial high-sensitivity troponin is correct — it is the biochemical gold standard for detecting myocardial necrosis and is the pivotal test in this scenario.
D-dimer is used to exclude pulmonary embolism, not to differentiate types of ACS.
Exercise stress testing is absolutely contraindicated in the acute setting of suspected ACS with ongoing symptoms and ECG changes.
clinicalPearl: The only difference between NSTEMI and unstable angina is a rise in troponin — serial high-sensitivity troponin is the pivotal investigation.
keyClue: Rest pain + ST depression/T-wave inversion on ECG — troponin result determines NSTEMI versus unstable angina.
commonExamTrap: Ordering an exercise stress test in a patient with ongoing chest pain and ECG changes — this is dangerous and contraindicated.
missedConcept: Serial troponin measurements (not a single value) are required because early samples may be falsely negative in acute MI.
redFlagReason: NSTEMI requires urgent antiplatelet therapy and consideration of early invasive strategy; misclassification as unstable angina may delay life-saving treatment.

clinicalStem: A 72-year-old male underwent primary PCI for an anterior STEMI 2 days ago. He now develops sudden-onset breathlessness and hypotension. On examination: BP 80/50 mmHg, HR 118 bpm, JVP elevated, bilateral crepitations at both lung bases, SpO2 88% on air. He is cold and clammy peripherally. Bedside echocardiogram shows severely impaired LV function with an EF of 20% and no pericardial effusion. What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
explanation: Cardiogenic shock following extensive anterior STEMI presents with hypotension, tachycardia, signs of low cardiac output (cold peripheries, oliguria), and signs of pulmonary congestion (crepitations, low SpO2). The echocardiogram confirming severely impaired LV function (EF 20%) with no pericardial effusion and no VSD clinches the diagnosis. This is the most feared mechanical complication of large anterior MI.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Pulmonary embolism can cause hypotension and breathlessness but would show right heart strain on echo, not severely impaired LV function; it also lacks bilateral crepitations from pulmonary oedema.
Tension pneumothorax causes tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds unilaterally, and distended neck veins — not bilateral crepitations or low LV EF on echo.
Cardiogenic shock is correct — the combination of post-anterior MI, severely impaired LV function, hypotension, and pulmonary oedema is the classic picture.

VSD complicating MI causes a new loud pansystolic murmur at the left sternal edge and a step-up in oxygen saturation from right atrium to pulmonary artery; the echo would show an interventricular defect.
Cardiac tamponade would show a pericardial effusion on echo with diastolic collapse of the right heart chambers — the echo here explicitly excludes this.
clinicalPearl: Cardiogenic shock post-large MI = hypotension + pulmonary oedema + cold peripheries + severely reduced LV EF on echo — mortality exceeds 50% without urgent revascularisation and haemodynamic support.
keyClue: Severely impaired LV (EF 20%) with no pericardial effusion, post-anterior STEMI, with hypotension and pulmonary oedema.
commonExamTrap: Confusing cardiogenic shock with cardiac tamponade — both cause hypotension and elevated JVP, but tamponade shows pericardial effusion on echo, not LV dysfunction.
missedConcept: Mechanical complications of MI (VSD, free-wall rupture, papillary muscle rupture, cardiogenic shock) can occur hours to days post-MI and must be distinguished by echo.
redFlagReason: Cardiogenic shock carries >50% mortality and requires immediate intensive haemodynamic support (vasopressors, intra-aortic balloon pump, or mechanical circulatory support) and urgent revascularisation.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old male with a 3-month history of exertional chest tightness presents for evaluation. The pain occurs predictably on climbing two flights of stairs and resolves within 5 minutes of rest. He has no pain at rest. He has hypertension and a 20-pack-year smoking history. Resting ECG is normal. BP 148/86 mmHg. He is able to exercise. What is the MOST APPROPRIATE initial non-invasive investigation to confirm the diagnosis of obstructive coronary artery disease?
explanation: In a patient who can exercise adequately and has a normal resting ECG, exercise ECG (treadmill stress test) remains the first-line non-invasive functional test for evaluating suspected stable angina due to obstructive CAD. It is widely available, low cost, and provides prognostic information (Duke Treadmill Score). A positive result (≥1 mm horizontal or downsloping ST depression at peak exercise) is highly suggestive of ischaemia. Functional testing is preferred over anatomical testing as the initial step in patients who can exercise with a normal baseline ECG.
wrongAnswerExplanations: CCTA is an anatomical test that identifies stenosis but does not directly assess ischaemia; it is often used when functional testing is inconclusive or in intermediate pre-test probability patients where anatomy is needed.
Resting echocardiogram shows baseline LV function and wall motion at rest but will not provoke ischaemia and is not the primary test to confirm stable angina.
Exercise ECG is correct — it is the first-line functional test for a patient who can exercise and has a normal baseline ECG.
Stress cardiac MRI is an excellent test for ischaemia but is more expensive, less widely available, and is reserved for patients who cannot exercise or have uninterpretable ECGs.
A resting nuclear scan provides no ischaemia information without stress; stress nuclear imaging is reserved for those with baseline ECG abnormalities or inability to exercise.
clinicalPearl: Exercise ECG is the first-line test for stable angina in patients who can exercise and have a normal resting ECG — it is cheap, widely available, and provides ischaemia confirmation plus prognostic data.
keyClue: Exertional chest pain relieved by rest, normal resting ECG, and the patient can exercise — ideal candidate for exercise ECG.
commonExamTrap: Jumping to coronary angiography without first confirming ischaemia non-invasively in a stable outpatient.
missedConcept: Non-invasive functional testing should precede invasive coronary angiography in stable patients to confirm haemodynamically significant ischaemia.

clinicalStem: A 68-year-old woman with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes and hypertension presents for routine review. She denies chest pain. Her GP notes she has been more breathless on exertion over the past 3 months and has had two episodes of unexplained diaphoresis. Resting ECG shows non-specific T-wave changes in V4-V6. Echocardiogram reveals mild inferior wall hypokinesis with EF 50%. What is the MOST LIKELY underlying diagnosis?
options: Hypertensive heart disease with diastolic dysfunction
Silent myocardial ischaemia due to chronic coronary artery disease
explanation: Diabetic patients frequently develop silent myocardial ischaemia due to autonomic neuropathy blunting the pain response — they may present with atypical symptoms such as exertional dyspnoea, diaphoresis, or fatigue rather than classic chest pain. The inferior wall hypokinesis on echo suggests a fixed or reversible ischaemic territory (right coronary artery territory). This clinical picture — atypical symptoms, T-wave changes, regional wall motion abnormality — is highly characteristic of silent ischaemia in the context of chronic coronary syndrome.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Hypertensive heart disease causes concentric LV hypertrophy and diastolic dysfunction but does not typically cause regional wall motion abnormalities such as inferior hypokinesis.
Diabetic cardiomyopathy is a diffuse process causing symmetric LV dysfunction, not regional wall motion abnormalities — it would not explain inferior-specific hypokinesis.
Silent myocardial ischaemia is correct — regional wall motion abnormality (inferior hypokinesis) in a diabetic patient with atypical symptoms and T-wave changes points to ischaemic aetiology.
Takotsubo cardiomyopathy causes apical ballooning (apical wall motion abnormality) typically after an emotional or physical stressor, not isolated inferior hypokinesis.
Dilated cardiomyopathy causes global LV dilation and reduced EF with generalised, not regional, wall motion abnormality.
clinicalPearl: Diabetics can have myocardial ischaemia without chest pain (silent ischaemia) due to autonomic neuropathy — regional wall motion abnormality on echo is a key diagnostic clue.
keyClue: Long-standing diabetes + exertional dyspnoea without chest pain + inferior wall hypokinesis on echo = silent ischaemia.
commonExamTrap: Attributing all cardiac findings in diabetics to 'diabetic cardiomyopathy' without considering silent ischaemia, which is the more treatable and specific diagnosis.
missedConcept: Silent myocardial ischaemia is common in diabetics; regional wall motion abnormality on echo should always prompt evaluation for obstructive CAD.

clinicalStem: A 62-year-old male with known stable angina has been on optimal medical therapy with a beta-blocker, long-acting nitrate, and aspirin for 6 months. Despite this, he continues to have exertional chest pain at low workloads (walking 100 metres on flat ground). His exercise ECG shows 3 mm ST depression at 4 METs. His resting ECG and echocardiogram are normal. What is the MOST APPROPRIATE next investigation?
options: Add a calcium channel blocker and reassess in 3 months
explanation: This patient has refractory stable angina — symptoms persisting at low workloads despite optimal medical therapy (OMT). The high-risk exercise ECG findings (≥2 mm ST depression at low workload <5 METs) indicate significant myocardial ischaemia. In patients with OMT failure and high-risk non-invasive findings, invasive coronary angiography is indicated to define coronary anatomy and assess suitability for revascularisation (PCI or CABG). This is a Class I indication in current ESC guidelines.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Adding a calcium channel blocker is reasonable in some patients, but this patient already has high-risk ischaemia markers (≥2 mm ST depression at very low workload) warranting anatomical assessment, not further medical optimisation alone.

CCTA provides anatomical information but is less useful when functional testing already demonstrates significant high-risk ischaemia requiring interventional decision-making; direct invasive angiography is preferred in this context.
Invasive coronary angiography is correct — refractory angina despite OMT with high-risk stress test is a guideline indication for invasive assessment and potential revascularisation.
Dobutamine stress echo would add further functional information but is redundant when high-risk ischaemia has already been demonstrated; the next step is anatomy and revascularisation planning.
A 24-hour Holter is used for arrhythmia evaluation, not for assessing coronary artery disease or guiding revascularisation decisions.
clinicalPearl: Refractory angina despite optimal medical therapy + high-risk stress test (≥2 mm ST depression at low workload) = indication for invasive coronary angiography to plan revascularisation.
keyClue: Persistent angina at very low workloads despite maximal medical therapy plus 3 mm ST depression at 4 METs.
commonExamTrap: Continuing to add more medications when the patient already has high-risk non-invasive findings that mandate coronary anatomy definition.
missedConcept: High-risk features on non-invasive testing (early onset ST depression, large territory, low workload) are indications for invasive angiography regardless of symptom severity.

clinicalStem: A 70-year-old woman with long-standing hypertension and atrial fibrillation presents with a 6-week history of progressive exertional breathlessness and ankle swelling. She denies orthopnoea. On examination: BP 160/95 mmHg, HR irregular at 90 bpm, JVP elevated 5 cm above the angle of Louis, mild bilateral pitting oedema to mid-shin, bibasal crepitations. Echocardiogram shows: LV wall thickness 13 mm (increased), LV cavity normal size, EF 62%, diastolic filling pattern: E/A ratio < 0.8, elevated E/e' ratio of 16. What type of heart failure does this patient MOST LIKELY have?
options: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF)
Heart failure with mildly reduced ejection fraction (HFmrEF)
Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF)
explanation: HFpEF is defined as heart failure symptoms and signs with a preserved EF (≥50%) and evidence of diastolic dysfunction or elevated filling pressures. This patient has clinical heart failure (dyspnoea, ankle oedema, elevated JVP) with an EF of 62%, LV hypertrophy from longstanding hypertension, and diastolic dysfunction indices (E/A < 0.8, elevated E/e' > 15). Hypertension and AF are the most common causes of HFpEF. Treatment focuses on managing comorbidities and controlling fluid.
wrongAnswerExplanations: HFrEF requires EF < 40%. This patient has EF 62%, excluding HFrEF.
HFmrEF is defined as EF 40-49%. This patient's EF of 62% falls in the preserved range.
HFpEF is correct — preserved EF ≥50% with clinical heart failure and echo evidence of diastolic dysfunction (elevated E/e' ratio, LV hypertrophy) in a hypertensive patient.

High-output heart failure occurs in conditions like anaemia, thyrotoxicosis, and arteriovenous fistulae — it would present with warm peripheries, wide pulse pressure, and a hyperkinetic circulation, none of which are present here.

Restrictive cardiomyopathy can mimic HFpEF but typically shows a specific pattern of biatrial enlargement, thickened myocardium with a 'sparkle' pattern, and more severe diastolic dysfunction — requires further workup to distinguish.
clinicalPearl: HFpEF = heart failure symptoms + EF ≥50% + evidence of diastolic dysfunction on echo; hypertension and AF are the leading causes in elderly women.
keyClue: EF 62% (preserved) + diastolic dysfunction indices (E/A < 0.8, E/e' 16) + LV hypertrophy in a hypertensive patient with heart failure symptoms.
commonExamTrap: Assuming all heart failure is HFrEF — preserved EF heart failure (HFpEF) is extremely common in elderly hypertensive patients and is diagnosed only by echo.
missedConcept: HFpEF is defined by preserved systolic function with impaired diastolic filling; echo diastolic parameters (E/A ratio, E/e' ratio) are essential for diagnosis.

clinicalStem: A 66-year-old male with a history of ischaemic heart disease presents to the outpatient clinic with a 4-week history of worsening breathlessness on exertion and two-pillow orthopnoea. He has had two emergency admissions in the past year for similar symptoms. On examination: bilateral pitting leg oedema, elevated JVP, S3 gallop. His resting ECG shows LBBB. His GP wants a single blood test that will best confirm the diagnosis of heart failure and assess its severity. Which test should be ordered?
explanation: BNP (B-type natriuretic peptide) and NT-proBNP are released by ventricular myocytes in response to increased wall stress and volume overload. They are highly sensitive biomarkers for heart failure, with a high negative predictive value (a normal BNP effectively excludes significant heart failure). Elevated levels correlate with heart failure severity, NYHA class, and predict adverse outcomes. Current ESC guidelines recommend BNP/NT-proBNP as part of the diagnostic workup for suspected heart failure.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Full blood count may detect anaemia (which can worsen or mimic heart failure) but does not diagnose or quantify heart failure itself.
Serum BNP or NT-proBNP is correct — it is the guideline-recommended blood test for confirming heart failure and assessing severity.
Troponin is elevated in acute myocardial injury and can be mildly elevated in decompensated heart failure, but it is not the primary diagnostic test for heart failure.
CRP is a marker of inflammation and infection; it is not specific for heart failure.
Serum aldosterone is elevated in heart failure (RAAS activation) but is not used as a diagnostic or severity marker in clinical practice.
clinicalPearl: BNP/NT-proBNP is the most important blood biomarker for diagnosing and stratifying heart failure; a high NT-proBNP confirms the diagnosis and predicts mortality.
keyClue: A single blood test to confirm heart failure and assess severity — BNP/NT-proBNP is the answer.
commonExamTrap: Choosing troponin as the primary heart failure biomarker — troponin assesses myocardial injury, not ventricular wall stress.
missedConcept: BNP/NT-proBNP are released specifically in response to ventricular wall stress and volume overload, making them the diagnostic biomarkers of choice in heart failure.

clinicalStem: A 74-year-old male with known heart failure (EF 30%) presents at 3 AM with sudden severe breathlessness. He is sitting bolt upright in bed, unable to speak in full sentences. On examination: RR 32/min, SpO2 82% on air, HR 112 bpm, BP 170/100 mmHg, bilateral coarse crepitations to mid-zones, pink frothy sputum. Chest X-ray shows bilateral perihilar shadowing, upper-lobe blood diversion, and small bilateral pleural effusions. What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
options: Bilateral pleural effusions secondary to malignancy
explanation: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema classically presents with sudden-onset nocturnal breathlessness (paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea), orthopnoea, frothy pink sputum, bilateral crackles, and the characteristic 'bat-wing' perihilar shadowing on CXR with upper-lobe blood diversion. The known severe LV dysfunction (EF 30%), hypertensive episode, and these CXR findings confirm acute decompensated heart failure with flash pulmonary oedema. Immediate management includes high-flow oxygen, IV furosemide, nitrates, and possibly NIV.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Community-acquired pneumonia can cause breathlessness and bilateral infiltrates, but not the characteristic CXR pattern of upper-lobe diversion, 'bat-wing' perihilar oedema, and pleural effusions together, nor is frothy pink sputum expected.
Pulmonary embolism causes sudden breathlessness but typically shows a clear or oligaemic CXR (Westermark sign), not bilateral perihilar oedema; it also does not produce frothy sputum.
Acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema is correct — frothy pink sputum, severe dyspnoea, perihilar 'bat-wing' CXR pattern, upper-lobe diversion, and known severe LV dysfunction all confirm this.
COPD exacerbation causes wheeze and hyperinflation; it does not produce frothy pink sputum or the perihilar oedema CXR pattern.
Malignant pleural effusions are typically exudative, build up slowly, and would not explain the acute onset, perihilar oedema pattern, or pink frothy sputum.
clinicalPearl: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema: sudden-onset orthopnoea, frothy pink sputum, bilateral crackles, and 'bat-wing' perihilar CXR shadowing with upper-lobe blood diversion in a patient with known LV dysfunction.
keyClue: Pink frothy sputum + bilateral perihilar CXR shadowing + upper-lobe diversion + known EF 30% = cardiogenic pulmonary oedema.
commonExamTrap: Attributing bilateral CXR changes to pneumonia without recognising the specific cardiogenic pattern (perihilar, upper-lobe diversion, Kerley B lines, pleural effusions).
missedConcept: The CXR pattern in cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (ABCDE: Alveolar oedema, Bat-wing, Cardiomegaly, Diversion of upper-lobe vessels, Effusions) is distinct from infectious consolidation.
redFlagReason: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema with SpO2 82% is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate oxygen, IV furosemide, nitrates, and consideration of non-invasive ventilation.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman presents to the ED with a 24-hour history of right-sided pleuritic chest pain and mild breathlessness. She had a long-haul flight (14 hours) 5 days ago and is currently taking the combined oral contraceptive pill. On examination: HR 102 bpm, RR 20/min, SpO2 96% on air, BP 118/74 mmHg. There is no clinical calf tenderness. Chest auscultation is unremarkable. ECG shows sinus tachycardia. What is the BEST next diagnostic step?
options: Calculate clinical probability score (Wells), then D-dimer if low probability
explanation: Current NICE/ESC guidelines recommend a structured diagnostic algorithm for suspected PE: first calculate the clinical pre-test probability using the Wells PE score. If low probability, perform a D-dimer — a negative D-dimer effectively rules out PE and avoids unnecessary radiation. If high probability, or if D-dimer is positive, proceed to CTPA. This patient has risk factors (OCP, long-haul flight) and pleuritic chest pain — the Wells score quantifies probability before committing to CTPA radiation. A structured approach is preferred over immediate CTPA in non-high-risk patients.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Immediate CTPA without risk stratification exposes the patient to unnecessary radiation and contrast if D-dimer can safely exclude PE in a low-probability patient; it is reserved for high-probability cases or haemodynamically unstable patients.
Calculating clinical probability (Wells score) followed by D-dimer if low probability is correct — this is the guideline-recommended, structured diagnostic pathway for suspected PE.

V/Q scan is an alternative to CTPA (preferred in young women due to lower breast radiation dose) but should still be preceded by clinical probability assessment; it is also less available and requires a normal CXR.
Lower-limb Doppler may identify DVT but its absence does not exclude PE, and a positive finding would still require treatment but not directly diagnose PE.
ABG may show hypoxia/hypocapnia but is not diagnostic for PE and should not be the primary investigation; its result does not alter the diagnostic algorithm.
clinicalPearl: Suspected PE: first stratify with Wells score — if low probability, do D-dimer; if high probability or positive D-dimer, proceed to CTPA. Never skip risk stratification.
keyClue: Risk factors for PE (OCP + long-haul flight) + pleuritic chest pain + tachycardia — structured risk stratification (Wells score) before investigation.
commonExamTrap: Ordering CTPA immediately without first performing clinical risk stratification — avoidable radiation if D-dimer could safely exclude PE in a low-probability patient.
missedConcept: The Wells score stratifies pre-test probability, and D-dimer has a high negative predictive value — together they can safely exclude PE without CTPA in low-probability patients.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old male is brought to the resuscitation bay with sudden collapse. His wife reports he had right calf pain and swelling for 10 days. On examination he is confused and agitated; BP 72/40 mmHg, HR 130 bpm, RR 28/min, SpO2 80% on 15L oxygen via non-rebreather mask. JVP is markedly elevated. ECG shows sinus tachycardia with S1Q3T3 pattern. Bedside echo shows right ventricular dilation, reduced RV function, and D-shaped left ventricle. What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
explanation: Massive (high-risk) PE presents with haemodynamic instability (systolic BP <90 mmHg or drop ≥40 mmHg) caused by acute right heart obstruction. The clinical picture is classic: preceding DVT symptoms, sudden collapse, refractory hypoxia, markedly elevated JVP (RV failure), S1Q3T3 on ECG (right heart strain), and bedside echo showing RV dilation with D-sign (interventricular septum bowing into LV due to RV pressure overload). This is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate systemic thrombolysis if no contraindications.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Acute severe asthma causes wheeze, prolonged expiration, and hyperinflation on CXR; it does not cause RV dilation, D-sign on echo, or S1Q3T3 on ECG.
Tension pneumothorax causes tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds unilaterally, and circulatory collapse, but echo would not show RV dilation with D-sign; there would be no DVT history.
Massive PE is correct — haemodynamic collapse, DVT history, S1Q3T3 ECG, and echo showing RV dilation and D-sign are the hallmarks of high-risk PE.
Anterior STEMI with cardiogenic shock would show anterior ST elevation on ECG and severely impaired LV (not RV) function on echo; S1Q3T3 pattern is not a feature.
Septic shock causes vasodilatory hypotension with warm peripheries and would not produce the specific RV dilation pattern or S1Q3T3 on ECG.
clinicalPearl: Massive PE = haemodynamic instability + RV dilation on echo (D-sign) + S1Q3T3 on ECG + preceding DVT symptoms. Requires immediate systemic thrombolysis (alteplase) in the absence of contraindications.
keyClue: Preceding calf DVT + haemodynamic collapse + elevated JVP + S1Q3T3 + RV dilation with D-sign on echo = massive PE.
commonExamTrap: Confusing cardiogenic shock (LV failure) with obstructive shock (massive PE causing RV failure) — echo finding of RV dilation with D-sign is the key differentiator.
missedConcept: Massive PE causes obstructive shock via acute RV outflow obstruction; echo showing RV dilation and D-sign is the bedside diagnostic key when CTPA is not feasible.
redFlagReason: Massive PE with haemodynamic collapse and SpO2 80% is immediately life-threatening; systemic thrombolysis (alteplase) is the first-line treatment and must not be delayed.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old woman who is 6 weeks post-caesarean section presents with a 3-day history of left calf pain, swelling, and redness. On examination: left calf circumference is 3 cm greater than the right, the calf is tender to palpation, and there is mild pitting oedema. She had no recent trauma. Her D-dimer is elevated at 1.8 mg/L. What is the MOST APPROPRIATE next investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
options: CT venography of the lower limbs
Venous duplex (compression) ultrasound of the left leg
explanation: Venous duplex ultrasound (compression ultrasound) of the affected limb is the first-line imaging investigation for suspected DVT. It is non-invasive, radiation-free, widely available, and has high sensitivity (>95%) and specificity for proximal DVT. Compression ultrasound involves applying pressure over the venous segment — a non-compressible vein indicates thrombus. The positive D-dimer combined with the clinical features strongly suggests DVT, and compression ultrasound will confirm or exclude it. D-dimer elevation post-partum is expected and not specific.
wrongAnswerExplanations: CT venography provides excellent anatomical detail but is not the first-line test due to radiation and contrast exposure; it is reserved for cases where ultrasound is technically inadequate.
MRI is occasionally used for suspected pelvic or iliac vein thrombosis but is not the standard initial investigation for calf DVT.
Venous duplex (compression) ultrasound is correct — it is the gold-standard first-line imaging for suspected lower limb DVT.
Repeating the D-dimer is unhelpful as it is already elevated; the next step is imaging to confirm or exclude thrombus.
CTPA is performed when pulmonary embolism is suspected; this patient has symptoms localised to the calf with no respiratory symptoms, so CTPA is not the immediate next step.
clinicalPearl: Venous compression ultrasound is the first-line investigation for suspected lower limb DVT — non-compressibility of the vein confirms thrombus.
keyClue: Unilateral calf swelling, tenderness, erythema post-partum with elevated D-dimer — needs imaging confirmation with compression ultrasound.
commonExamTrap: Stopping at D-dimer — an elevated D-dimer in a high-probability patient requires imaging (compression ultrasound), not further blood tests.
missedConcept: D-dimer has a high negative predictive value to exclude DVT in low-probability patients but a positive D-dimer always requires imaging confirmation, not repeat testing.
